m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following problem solving technique used in binary search
m_Item1.Explanation: It Work with divid and conquer approach.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In which of the follwing sorting algorithm elements which are at two consecutive positions gets compared for sorting.
m_Item1.Explanation: Bubble sort works like consecutive positions.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following best describes the time complexity of a linear search algorithm in the worst case scenario?
m_Item1.Explanation: In a linear search, each element in the collection has to be checked until the desired element is found or all elements have been checked, resulting in a time complexity of O(n) in the worst case.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Consider an unsorted array of size n. If the target element is not present in the array, how many comparisons would a linear search make?
m_Item1.Explanation: In a linear search, the algorithm runs through the entire array to determine that the target element is not present, resulting in n comparisons.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the primary advantage of using a linear search algorithm?

Options.OptionText: It can quickly find elements in large, sorted data structures.
It is faster than binary search in most cases.
It requires no additional memory allocation apart from the input data.
It guarantees constant time complexity for all data structures.

m_Item1.Explanation: Linear search operates directly on the input data without needing additional data structures, making it memory efficient in terms of not requiring extra space.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In what scenario is a linear search more practical compared to other search algorithms like binary search?

Options.OptionText: When the dataset is very large and sorted.
When the dataset is small and unsorted.
When frequent insertions and deletions are required.
When the dataset is static and sorted.

m_Item1.Explanation: A linear search is more practical on small, unsorted datasets due to its simplicity and the overhead of sorting not being justified for small data volumes.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following characteristics does not apply to the linear search algorithm?

Options.OptionText: It works on both sorted and unsorted arrays.
It uses a divide and conquer approach.
It has a linear time complexity in the average case.
It fails if the array is empty.

m_Item1.Explanation: Linear search does not use a divide and conquer approach; this approach is typical of algorithms like binary search. Linear search simply checks each element one by one.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the worst-case time complexity of the linear search algorithm?
m_Item1.Explanation: The worst-case time complexity of the linear search algorithm is O(n) because in the worst case, each element in the array needs to be checked.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will the following code snippet print?

int[] arr = {10, 20, 30, 40};
int x = 20;
boolean found = false;
for (int i = 0; i < arr.length; i++) {
    if (arr[i] == x) {
        System.out.println("Element found");
        found = true;
        break;
    }
}
if (!found) {
    System.out.println("Element not found");
}
m_Item1.Explanation: The snippet searches for the element '20' in the array and prints "Element found" once it is located.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which statement is true about linear search?

Options.OptionText: It only works on sorted arrays.
It can find multiple occurrences of the target value.
It is always faster than binary search.

m_Item1.Explanation: Linear search can find multiple occurrences of a target value as it checks each element of the array sequentially.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Which of the following code snippets correctly implements a linear search method to find element 'key' in array 'arr'?

int[] arr = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5};
int key = 3;
System.out.println(linearSearch(arr, key));

Options.OptionText: int linearSearch(int[] arr, int key) { for (int i = 0; i <= arr.length; i++) { if (arr[i] == key) return i; } return -1; }
int linearSearch(int[] arr, int key) { for (int i = 0; i < arr.length; i++) { if (arr[i] == key) return i; } return -1; }
int linearSearch(int[] arr, int key) { for (int i = 0; i < arr.length - 1; i++) { if (arr[i] == key) return i; } return arr[0]; }
int linearSearch(int[] arr, int key) { for (int i : arr) { if (arr[i] == key) return arr[i]; } return -1; }

m_Item1.Explanation: Option B is correct because it checks each element of the array from index 0 to arr.length - 1, and returns the index if the key is found.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the worst-case time complexity of the sequential search algorithm?
m_Item1.Explanation: In the sequential search algorithm, in the worst-case scenario, you have to check each element of the array or list once. If the desired element is at the last position or not present in the list at all, it results in linear time complexity, O(n).

m_Item1.QuestionText: Given a list of n unsorted elements, how many comparisons are needed on average to find a target element using sequential search?
m_Item1.Explanation: The average number of comparisons in a sequential search is n/2 because each element has an equal chance of being the target, so on average, half of the elements need to be checked.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the primary disadvantage of using a sequential search algorithm?

Options.OptionText: It requires the list to be sorted
It is inefficient for large lists
It requires additional space beyond the input elements
It can only be used for lists of numbers

m_Item1.Explanation: The sequential search algorithm has O(n) time complexity making it inefficient for large lists compared to more efficient search algorithms like binary search on sorted lists.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In the context of data structures and Java, which Java interface would you most likely use if you were to implement a sequential search?
m_Item1.Explanation: The Iterable interface would be used in Java to traverse collections, which is often necessary when implementing a sequential search to go through elements in a list or collection.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is true about the time complexity of a binary search algorithm in the worst-case scenario?
m_Item1.Explanation: In the worst-case scenario, the time complexity of a binary search is O(log n) because the search space is reduced by half with each iteration.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which condition must be met before you can apply the binary search algorithm on a given array?

Options.OptionText: The array must contain distinct elements.
The array must not contain any duplicate values.
The array must have more than one element.

m_Item1.Explanation: Binary search requires the array to be sorted beforehand to efficiently reduce the search space by half during each step.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the primary advantage of using binary search over linear search?

Options.OptionText: Binary search works on both sorted and unsorted arrays.
Binary search has a lower time complexity in average cases.
Binary search can find multiple elements at once.

m_Item1.Explanation: Binary search has a lower time complexity O(log n) for average cases compared to linear search's O(n), but it requires the array to be sorted.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In a recursive implementation of a binary search, which scenario indicates that a search should terminate with a 'not found' status?

Options.OptionText: When the middle element is the search target.
When the search space is reduced to a single element.
When the left index exceeds the right index.
When an element is not present in the middle of the array.

m_Item1.Explanation: The binary search should terminate with 'not found' when the left index exceeds the right index, indicating the search space has been exhausted.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Given an array arr[] = {-10, -1, 0, 3, 5, 9, 12}, what will be the result of binary search for element 3?
m_Item1.Explanation: The binary search would locate the element '3' at index 3 in the sorted array.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will be the output of the following recursive binary search code snippet given the array 'numbers = {2, 3, 5, 8, 13, 21, 34}' and a target value 13?

public static int binarySearch(int[] arr, int left, int right, int target) {
    if (right >= left) {
        int mid = left + (right - left) / 2;
        if (arr[mid] == target)
            return mid;
        if (arr[mid] > target)
            return binarySearch(arr, left, mid - 1, target);
        return binarySearch(arr, mid + 1, right, target);
    }
    return -1;
}
public static void main(String[] args) {
    int[] numbers = {2, 3, 5, 8, 13, 21, 34};
    System.out.println(binarySearch(numbers, 0, numbers.length - 1, 13));
}
m_Item1.Explanation: The number 13 is present at index 4 of the given array. The binary search will return the index 4 as the target value 13 is found there.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
In the context of iterative binary search implementation, what is the purpose of the following line in the code?
int mid = left + (right - left) / 2;

Options.OptionText: To calculate the average of left and right.
To avoid integer overflow when computing the mid index.
To directly assign mid to the value of the target.
To initiate the sorting of the array.

m_Item1.Explanation: The line calculates the middle index of the array segment by using a strategy that avoids overflow, which can happen if the index sum exceeds the maximum value an integer can hold.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What condition should typically terminate a binary search loop or recursive function?

Options.OptionText: When the middle element equals the target value.
When left is greater than right.

m_Item1.Explanation: The search loop or recursion should terminate when the 'left' pointer surpasses the 'right' pointer, indicating that the target is not present in the array.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How can binary search be adapted to find the first occurrence of a duplicate target value in a sorted array?

Options.OptionText: Modify binary search to continue looking on both sides after finding the target.
Modify binary search to continue searching in the left half even after finding the target.
Use linear search after finding one occurrence with binary search.

m_Item1.Explanation: The search should continue towards the left even after finding an occurrence to ensure that the first occurrence is identified. This modification requires additional conditions to keep checking the left side of the array.

m_Item1.QuestionText: A characteristic of the data that binary search uses but the linear search ignores is the___________.

Options.OptionText: Order of the elements of the list.
Type of elements of the list.

m_Item1.Explanation: The correct characteristic is 1. Order of the elements of the list. Binary search critically depends on the sorted order of the data to function correctly and efficiently, whereas linear search does not make use of this ordering information.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Binary search algorithm cannot be applied to
m_Item1.Explanation: Binary search algorithm will be applied on linear data structure whereas, tree is non linear data structure.

m_Item1.QuestionText: The Average case occur in linear search algorithm

Options.OptionText: When Item is somewhere in the middle of the array
When Item is not in the array at all
When Item is the last element in the array
When Item is the last element in the array or is not there at all

m_Item1.Explanation: In average case, for searching item, average no of iteration should be completed for searching, & it is possible when item is present in somewhere on middle.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is not the required condition for binary search algorithm?

Options.OptionText: there should be the direct access to the middle element in any sublist
There must be mechanism to delete and/or insert elements in list
First element is smallest element in array

m_Item1.Explanation: In binary search the array should be in sorted form & we can get direct access to the middle element, & insertion/deletion is not required.

m_Item1.QuestionText: The average and worst case time complexity of Binary search algorithm is
m_Item1.Explanation:
ComplexityOf Binary Search   
Best Case Time Complexity of Binary Search: O(1)
Average Case Time Complexity of Binary Search: O(logN)
Worst Case Time Complexity of Binary Search: O(logN)

m_Item1.QuestionText: The Worst case occur in linear search algorithm when

Options.OptionText: Item is somewhere in the middle of the array
Item is available  in the array at all
Item is the lnot ast element in the array
Item is the last element in the array or is not there at all

m_Item1.Explanation: If element is not present, it will search till last index & it is a worst case.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Finding the location of the element with a given value is:
m_Item1.Explanation: Searching is use to find value at given location.

m_Item1.QuestionText: The average and worst case time complexity of Binary search algorithm is
m_Item1.Explanation:
ComplexityOf Binary Search     for best case is o(1)
for average is o(log n)
 for worst also it is o(1) / o(log n)

m_Item1.QuestionText: The complexity of searching an element from a set of n elements using Binary search algorithm is
m_Item1.Explanation: In Binary Search a sorted array by repeatedly dividing the search interval in half. Begin with an interval covering the whole array. If the value of the search key is less than the item in the middle of the interval, narrow the interval to the lower half. Otherwise, narrow it to the upper half. Repeatedly check until the value is found or the interval is empty. So Complexity is O(log n)
